title: CloudFront Cache Keys, Origin Requests, TTLs, and Invalidation · Language Lens: Ttl Revalidation Errors
summary: A cache key includes every viewer header, authorization is omitted, the origin receives different data than the cache varies on, and releases rely on global invalidation as routine deployment. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the ttl revalidation errors increment in cache-key, forwarding, and freshness contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal d…
objectives: Combine origin Cache-Control, minimum, default, maximum, stale behavior, validators, and error caching with content-change and privacy requirements.
Use origin request policy for additional required viewer data without unnecessary cache fragmentation or trust escalation.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Ttl Revalidation Errors · Theory Ttl Revalidation Errors
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 6409 during a compliance restore drill. Build deterministic Go evidence to align cache identity, origin forwarding, TTL, versioned names, and urgent invalidation; retain stable tenant and file identity without exposing it in metric labels; then cancel halfway through and prove every body, part, and goroutine reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Ttl Revalidation Errors contract for the supplied data.
